claim: Benchmark of five claimed pipeline optimizations on the current codebase + Claude Code sandbox hardware (no GPU). Three are measured in-sandbox; two require Colab GPU and are recorded as plans with concrete invocations. (1) Gillespie Rust backend is 1.86x faster than pure Python on the RealSimulator.run hot-path (20-gene sample at scale=0.05, t_end=0.5s, dt=0.05s, seed=42): python mean 12.92s +/- 0.60s, rust mean 6.94s +/- 0.43s. (2) XGBoost tree_method=hist on CPU: 12.5s for 5-fold CV on the Tier-1 feature matrix (455x1295); GPU awaits Colab, published estimate 2-10x speedup. (3) Feature assembly pandas+pyarrow vs polars on synthetic 455x1295 float32 matrix (5% NaN in MACE cols to mimic coverage): pandas 67.1ms mean, polars 52.0ms mean = 1.29x speedup, but polars shows much higher variance (stdev 32.1ms vs 6.5ms), suggesting cold-start dominates on the small matrix. (4,5) ESM-2 batch-size scan (8/16/32/64) and ESM-2 150M vs 650M model comparison both require GPU; plans written for Colab execution with exact cell-by-cell invocations.
source_detail: Reproducible via scripts/bench_rust_vs_python.py (Rust speedup, measured), scripts/bench_xgboost_treemethod.py (XGBoost CPU, measured), scripts/bench_feature_assembly.py (feature assembly, measured), scripts/bench_esm2_batch.py (ESM-2 batch, plan-only in sandbox), scripts/bench_esm2_sizes.py (ESM-2 150M vs 650M, plan-only). Outputs under outputs/bench_*.json. Sandbox hardware: x86_64 Linux, Python 3.11, no CUDA. Seed 42 throughout.

esm2_batch_size_sensitivity.reason_not_measured: ESM-2 650M fp32 forward pass on a 300-residue protein takes tens of seconds on CPU; a CPU batch-size scan would take hours and would not reflect GPU inference patterns.

esm2_model_size_comparison.decision_rule: 150M is the better default if tier1_only MCC >= 0.12 AND union MCC >= 0.42; otherwise 650M stays.
esm2_model_size_comparison.blocking_codechange: ESM2Extractor hardcodes model_id as a class constant; needs a kwarg or a subclass before 150M can be extracted without duplicating the whole class.

xgboost_tree_method.gpu_published_estimate_source: xgboost release notes + dmlc/xgboost benchmark suite 2023-2024; typical speedup for small-matrix hist (n<=10k rows) is 2-5x; our 455-row matrix likely at the low end of that range.

feature_assembly.note: polars shows 1.29x mean speedup but ~5x stdev; the small matrix is in the cold-start regime where polars hasn't amortized its setup cost. On a 10k-row multi-organism matrix the gap is expected to widen in polars' favor.

caveats: Two of five benchmarks (ESM-2 batch sizing, ESM-2 150M vs 650M) require GPU and cannot be measured in the Claude Code sandbox. This fact file records in-sandbox measurements plus explicit plans for Colab measurement; no GPU numbers are manufactured.
The XGBoost GPU-hist benchmark similarly awaits Colab; its published-estimate speedup range (2-10x) should NOT be cited as a measured result. Overwrite with measured numbers after a Colab run.

No optimization in this fact file is integrated into the production pipeline. Integration is a separate decision per optimization: the Rust backend is already integrated and confirmed; the other four are advisory only.

The Rust speedup of 1.86x is below the 'roughly 2x' claim that prior work reported; re-verified here on a clean 20-gene sample at the same config as the production sweep. It is NOT the 10x that compiled-language rewrites of naive Python typically give, because the current FastEventSimulator is already using numpy-vectorised math and the remaining hot-path is limited by stochastic rule-sampling overhead rather than arithmetic.

The feature-assembly benchmark uses synthetic parquets (same shape + NaN pattern as Tier-1) rather than the real cache files, so we can iterate 10x without repeated disk-cache effects. Real-cache numbers would show higher variance due to filesystem caching.

XGBoost CV MCC measured here (0.2405) differs from the main train_tier1_xgboost.py result (0.1452) for the tier1 config; the main script uses the exact same hyperparameters and 5-fold split so the discrepancy likely comes from a small difference in how NaN is handled in this benchmark's path (no explicit label vector construction call). This file should NOT be treated as a replacement for the headline MCC measurements — it is a training-time benchmark only.
